coredata.dc:title: Unlocking the potential of carbon dots in agriculture using data-driven approaches

coredata.dc:description: The utilization of carbon dots (CDs) in agriculture to enhance plant growth has gained significant attention, but the data remains fractionated. Systematically integrating existing data is needed to identify the factors driving the interactions between CDs and plants and strategically guide future research. Articles reporting on CDs and their effects on plants were searched based on inclusion and exclusion criteria, resulting in the collection of 71 articles comprising a total of 2564 data points. The meta-analysis reveals that the soil and foliar application of red-emitting bio-derived CDs at a low concentration (<10 ppm) leads to the most beneficial effects on plant growth. Random forest and gradient boosting algorithms revealed that the size and dose of CDs were important factors in predicting plant responses across multiple aspects (CDs properties, plant properties, environmental factors, and experimental conditions). Specifically, smaller sizes are more favorable to growth indicators (GI) below 6 nm, nutrient and quality (NuQ) at 3–6 nm, photosynthesis (PSN) below 7 nm, and antioxidant responses (AR) below 5 nm. Overall, our analysis of existing data suggests that CDs applications can significantly improve plant responses (GI, NuQ, PSN, and AR) by 10–39 %. To unlock the full potential of CDs, customized synthesis techniques should be employed to meet the specific requirements of different crops and climate condition. For example, we recommend the synthesis of small CDs (<7 nm) with emission peak values falling within the range of 405–475 and 610–670 nm to enhance plant growth. The global prediction of plant responses to CDs application in future scenarios have shown significant improvements ranging from 17 to 58 %, suggesting that CDs have widespread applicability. This novel understanding of the impact of CDs on plant response provides valuable insights for optimizing the application of these nanomaterials in agriculture.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Visualization of the data distribution of the raw dataset using the ‘tabplot’ package in R software. Each column represents a variable, and each row bin is an aggregate of 26 records. Numeric variables are depicted using a bar chart displaying the mean values, while categorical variables are represented by a stacked bar chart illustrating the proportions of categories. Samples are arranged in descending order of the response index. Mean.e, mean value of effect size with CDs treatment; mean.c, mean value of effect size with control treatment; BDCDs, bio-derived carbon dots; HCDs, heterogeneous carbon dots; FA, foliar application; Hyd, hydroponic; Irr, irrigation; SA, soil application; NT, night temperature; DT, daytime temperature.

Plant responses to (a) types of CDs, (b) PL color, (c) application method, and (d) application level. The result with one to three asterisk (*, **, ***) refers to p < 0.05, p < 0.01, and p < 0.001, respectively. The bars represent the 95 % CI. The numbers between parentheses represent the number of paired samples used.

Performance and interpretation of the RF model. Actual versus predicted for plant response of (a) GI and (b) PSN, with cyan markers as the training set and orange markers as the test set. The SHAP importance of variables in informing the RF model for predicting (c) GI and (d) PSN. The positive or negative SHAP values (X-axis) indicate feature values and their relative impact (increase or decrease) on the prediction of plant response. All data points are color-coded according to the variable values they represent.

Feature importance and interaction analysis based on updated ML models. Important features across different plant responses with (a) RF models and (b) GB modes. Ill, illumination; Abs.s, single absorption peak; Ems.s, single emission peak; DT, daytime temperature; Hum, humidity; Str, Stress; GT, growth.time; Pho, photoperiod; In.G, Index group. Double-variable partial dependence for the size and dose in the prediction of (c) GI, (d) NuQ, (e) PSN, and (f) AR in RF model.

Global spatial patterns of plant response regulated by CDs. Left is the current scenario with (a) GI; (c) NuQ; (e) PSN; (g) AR. Right is the future scenario with (b) GI; (d) NuQ; (f) PSN; (h) AR. The white areas indicate no crop land data and were therefore not included in the global prediction.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of carbon dots (CDs) in agriculture to enhance plant growth has gained significant attention, but the data remains fractionated. Systematically integrating existing data is needed to identify the factors driving the interactions between CDs and plants and strategically guide future research. Articles reporting on CDs and their effects on plants were searched based on inclusion and exclusion criteria, resulting in the collection of 71 articles comprising a total of 2564 data points. The meta-analysis reveals that the soil and foliar application of red-emitting bio-derived CDs at a low concentration (<10 ppm) leads to the most beneficial effects on plant growth. Random forest and gradient boosting algorithms revealed that the size and dose of CDs were important factors in predicting plant responses across multiple aspects (CDs properties, plant properties, environmental factors, and experimental conditions). Specifically, smaller sizes are more favorable to growth indicators (GI) below 6 nm, nutrient and quality (NuQ) at 3–6 nm, photosynthesis (PSN) below 7 nm, and antioxidant responses (AR) below 5 nm. Overall, our analysis of existing data suggests that CDs applications can significantly improve plant responses (GI, NuQ, PSN, and AR) by 10–39 %. To unlock the full potential of CDs, customized synthesis techniques should be employed to meet the specific requirements of different crops and climate condition. For example, we recommend the synthesis of small CDs (<7 nm) with emission peak values falling within the range of 405–475 and 610–670 nm to enhance plant growth. The global prediction of plant responses to CDs application in future scenarios have shown significant improvements ranging from 17 to 58 %, suggesting that CDs have widespread applicability. This novel understanding of the impact of CDs on plant response provides valuable insights for optimizing the application of these nanomaterials in agriculture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The global cropland data are from NASA (https://lpdaac.usgs.gov/products/gfsad30valv001/). The illumination intensity, daytime and night temperatures are from NASA (https://neo.gsfc.nasa.gov/). The relative humidity is from Climate Data Store (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). The soil pH is from SoilGrids (https://www.soilgrids.org/).

ce:section.ce:para: Climate changes, increasing populations, and declining soil quality are posing challenges to global food security (Willett et al., 2019). Conventional agriculture falls short in promoting sustainable agricultural production (Lowry et al., 2019). It is essential to explore new strategies for a high-yielding and sustainable agricultural system to effectively tackle the rising problem of food insecurity. Carbon dots (CDs) are relatively novel carbon nanomaterials showing great potential for improving agricultural practices (Maholiya et al., 2023; Tripathi and Sarkar, 2022). CDs have been employed as nano-fertilizers (Liu et al., 2020), photosynthetic enhancers (Guo et al., 2022), and biotic and abiotic stress alleviators (Ragab and Saad-Allah, 2020). The biological effects of CDs depend on their properties, plant species, and soil characteristics (Cheng et al., 2023; Maholiya et al., 2023). For instance, the response of dicotyledonous and monocotyledonous plants was shown to differ, probably due to differences in vasculature and root structure (Maholiya et al., 2023). Li et al. also demonstrated that the different element doped CDs (300 μg/mL, Mg and N co-doped CDs) can increase 109.54 % photosynthetic activity and 127.16 % photosynthetic efficiency by causing a red-shift in photoluminescence (Li et al., 2021b). Despite the potential benefits of using CDs in agriculture, there are several challenges currently preventing the full understanding of the interactions between CDs and plants (Farooq et al., 2022). The effects of CDs depend on multiple factors, including CDs' properties, plant species, and soil characteristics (Maholiya et al., 2023). The complex nature of this interaction poses difficulties in identifying the specific mechanisms by which CDs affect plant growth and development (Hu and Xianyu, 2021). Most studies focus on isolated factors, making it difficult to achieve a comprehensive overview (Alotaibi et al., 2023; Hallaji et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021a). For instance, Wang et al. compared the fertilization effects of N-CDs at different concentrations (0, 0.2, or 4.0 mg/mL) and discovered that the 0.2 mg/mL concentration had the most significant beneficial impact on seed treatment, resulting in a 200 % increase in shoot and root lengths (Wang et al., 2019). Cheng et al. compared biomass-derived CDs doped with various nutrient elements (e.g., Mg, Cu, and Fe) and found that CDs could enhance photosynthetic activity through different mechanisms (Cheng et al., 2023). Fully understanding the influence of CDs on plant systems requires the consideration of the broader context and the interactions of multiple factors.

Data-driven methods such as machine learning (ML), data mining, statistical analysis, and meta-analysis can deal with these complexities (Osinga et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022), by unraveling data patterns, predicting future trends, and uncovering hidden knowledge within existing data (Liu et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023). This approach moves beyond smaller-scale empirical studies by enhancing statistical capabilities and summarization of findings (Dang et al., 2022). These methods have been successfully applied to explore the potential of nanomaterials in agriculture (Deng et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021b). For instance, Deng et al. used ML to construct reliable models (R2 > 0.8) for predicting plant responses and uptake/transport of nanomaterials, such as ZnO, Ag, CuO, and graphene (Deng et al., 2023). Yu et al. employed an interpretable ML method to gain insights into the uptake and translocation of nanomaterials in crop seedlings following seed treatments (Yu et al., 2023). Cui et al. used meta-analysis to study the influence of nanoscale zero-valent iron application on various physiological parameters of plant (Cui et al., 2023). Such approaches have not been applied to CDs yet. Given the large amount of experimental data recently generated on the interactions between CDs and plants, the data-driven approaches present an opportunity to extract meaningful and actionable insights to guide the development of suitable CDs for agriculture.

Therefore, this study aims to investigate the effects of CDs on four vital aspects of plant behavior: photosynthesis (PSN), nutrient content and quality (NuQ), growth indicators (GI), and antioxidant response (AR). This study employs a meta-analysis and ML approach to fill the existing knowledge gap regarding the global effectiveness and applicability of CDs in agriculture. Through our meta-analysis, the overall effect of CDs on plants is quantified, while ML enable the development of predictive models. Global prediction offers a more comprehensive understanding of the potential applicability of CDs nanotechnology on a worldwide scale, allowing for the identification of regions where the most favorable conditions for plant growth are expected based on climate and soil properties. The combination of these methodologies will enable the identification of key contributing factors, providing valuable insights for evidence-based decision-making in nano-enabled agriculture. Ultimately, the results of this study will deepen our understanding of the mechanisms underlying changes in crops controlled by the application of CDs and their interaction.

Peer-reviewed journal articles reporting on carbon dots were searched from the Web of Science (WOS, https://www.webofscience.com/wos/) and China National Knowledge Resource Integrated (CNKI, http://www.cnki.net) databases for articles published prior to April 20, 2024. Different combinations of keywords were employed as search terms, including “carbon dots” × “crop”, “carbon dots” × “plant”, “carbon dots” × “agriculture”. 1587 publications were initially identified, screened individually, to select 71 articles reporting original relevant data, comprising a total of 2564 data points. The selected articles met the following criteria: i) experiment results were reported as the mean value with standard deviation/error; ii) the only difference in experimental design between a pairwise comparison (control and treatment) was the application of carbon dots; and iii) the reported experiments results included at least three replications (Table S1). The data shown in figures were extracted using GetData Graph digitizer software (version 2.25).

A meta-analysis was conducted with R (metafor package) to explore the plant response to CDs and the influencing factors. The natural log-transformed response ratio (LnR) and associated variance were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2) for each observation

where Xt and Xc are the mean values of plant response index in the treatment and control, respectively; St and nt are the standard deviation and sample size for the treatment, respectively; and Sc and nc indicate the standard deviation and sample size for the control, respectively. The random-effects model was used to calculate the mean weight response ratio. The 95 % confidence interval (CI) did not overlap with zero, indicating the response ratio was significant (p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning model development and evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: To accurately and comprehensively test the relationships between CDs exposure characteristics and plant responses, the dataset was organized into 21 features, with each feature falling into one of four categories: CDs properties, plant properties, environmental factors, and experimental conditions. The four types of plant response, namely growth indicators, nutrient and quality, photosynthesis, and antioxidant responses, were selected as the output labels to represent the development potential of applying CDs in agriculture.

Before the model development, the input features were standardized using the StandardScaler from scikit-learn to ensure a similar scale and approximation to a normal distribution. The label encoding was used to overcome the problem of ML algorithms failing to recognize categorical columns. Several popular ML algorithms, including random forest (RF), gradient boosting (GB), support vector machine (SVM), and linear regression (LR) were used to predict the plant response of CDs. Specifically, the RF, GB, SVM, and LRmodels were built using the scikit-learn “RandomForestRegressor”, “GradientBoostingRegressor”, “SVR”, “LinearRegression” functions in Python 3.11, respectively. Grid search method was used to optimize the predictive performance by adjusted important parameters (such as ntree and mtry in RF). For model evaluation, the dataset was randomly divided into two parts: 80 % for training and the remaining 20 % for testing. Coefficient of determination (R2), mean squared error (MSE), root mean square error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE) were calculated to evaluate the performance and accuracy of ML models. Additionally, a 5-fold cross-validation method was used to detect the overfitting risk in model. The corresponding cross-validation coefficients (q2) were calculated using Eq. (3)
(3)
q 2
= 1 −

where yi is the predicted value, ŷ is the actual value, and y̅ is the average of the actual value. To simplify the ML model and enhance its performance, the sequential backward selection procedure was employed for feature selection. This process involves iteratively removing features from the model that have the least impact, aiming to reduce the dimensionality of the input features while preserving or improving the model performance.

The best-performance model was reconceptualized with a reduced set of 6 to 10 input features to obtain better generalization and higher computational efficiency (Cherkasov et al., 2014; Topliss, 1972). According to the update ML model results, three feature analysis methods were employed to assess the importance and interaction of features. The feature importance was directly determined through the final updated ML model. Besides, Shapley additive explanation (SHAP) method were used to rationalize the predictions of plant response and to quantify local contributions of input features to the specific prediction. To investigate the relationship between specific features and the model's predictions while holding other features constant, partial dependency plots (PDP) method was also applied.

The update ML prediction models were used to predict the potential of applying CDs in agriculture, taking into account climate and soil properties on a global scale. The main focus is to pinpoint regions where positive impacts on plant growth are most likely to occur, thereby providing clarity and reinforcement to the rationale behind the approach. The global cropland data were obtained from NASA (https://lpdaac.usgs.gov/products/gfsad30valv001/). The input data including illumination intensity, daytime and night temperatures were obtained from NASA (https://neo.gsfc.nasa.gov/). The relative humidity was obtained from Climate Data Store (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). The soil pH was obtained from SoilGrids (https://www.soilgrids.org/). For future predictions, data were obtained from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) of SSP126 (ESGF MetaGrid, https://aims2.llnl.gov/). Predicted values were mapped using ArcGIS 10.5. Given the result of meta-analysis, the red PL color with 1–10 ppm is beneficial for plant response. Hence, we assumed the CDs (5 nm, red PL color) at 10 ppm for global prediction.

Our rigorous literature-mining led to the identification of 71 peer-review publications between 2015 and early 2024, and reporting 2564 observations of plant response with CDs treatment. The list of references is provided in Table S1. The dataset includes plant responses categorized into four classes: photosynthesis, nutrient and quality, growth indicators, and antioxidant response. Furthermore, it encompasses 21 features grouped in four classes; i.e., CDs properties, plant properties, environmental factors, and experimental conditions (Table S2). The distribution of features was visualized in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1-S2. The type of CDs is determined by the source reagents used. In this dataset, heterogeneous carbon dots (HCDs, 46.9 %) are the most common, followed by bio-derived carbon dots (BDCDs, 30.3 %) and CDs (22.7 %). Traditional CDs are typically made from graphite and citric acid, occasionally with the addition of glucose. HCDs are synthesized using materials such as p-Phenylenediamine, trisodium citrate, reduced glutathione, a mix of citric acid with o-phenylenediamine, urea, ethanolamine, ethylenediamine, and others compounds. On the other hand, BDCDs are commonly derived from plant and animal materials such as beef, elephant manure, oranges, soybeans, walnuts, seaweed, peanut bran, Salvia Miltiorrhiza, Phellodendron chinense schneid, piper betel leaves, magnolia withered leaves, and pollens. Compared with CDs, HCDs and BDCDs often contain additional elements beyond C, H, O. These additional elements contribute to more functional groups and chemical diversity in HCDs and BDCDs, enhancing their properties and potential applications. The use of different raw materials results in the incorporation of various elements in CDs. For example, soybeans, walnuts, and seaweed may introduce Mg, Cu, and Fe into the CDs, respectively (Cheng et al., 2023). The most common synthesis method is hydrothermal (68.2 %), followed by microwave (19.7 %), electrolysis (2.4 %), and electrochemical (2.4 %). The photoluminescence colors (PL color) of the synthesized CDs are blue (75.1 %), yellow (8.6 %), and red (9.8 %). Quantum yield was also collected, but there was a substantial number of missing values (71.2 %) and thus this feature was excluded. Among the available data, 15.8 % of quantum yield are below 10.0 %, 42.9 % fall in the range of 10–20 %, 16.9 % fall in the range of 20–50 %, and 24.4 % have a quantum yield above 50 %. Regarding the application methods (AM), the majority of studies utilized hydroponic systems (44.8 %), followed by foliar application (33.4 %), irrigation (13.3 %), and a small proportion involved soil application (4.8 %) and seed treatment (3.5 %). In terms of dose, the distribution among the different levels was relatively even, with 29.3 % classified as low (≤10 ppm), 35.4 % as medium (10–100 ppm), 23.3 % as high (100–500 ppm), and 12.1 % as very high (>500 ppm). Commonly studied plants included lettuce (Lactuca sativa L., 33.1 %), maize (Zea mays L., 9.9 %), wheat (Triticum aestivum L.,7.4 %) cucumber (Cucumis sativus L., 5.9 %), tomato (Solanum lycopersicum L., 4.5 %), rice (Oryza sativa L., 4.2 %), mung beans (Vigna radiata L., 3.5 %), and soybean (Glycine max L., 3.1 %). The relatively even distribution of variables ensures a balanced representation of different factors, which is beneficial for subsequent data-driven methods (meta-analysis and ML).

CDs have significant impacts on plant growth and development. The results showed that different types of CDs possess distinct strengths in exerting positive effects on plants (Fig. 2a). Compared with CDs, BDCDs and HCDs exhibit greater enhancement of photosynthesis (BDCDs, +31.84 %, 95 % CI, 19.41–34.27 %; HCDs, +23.71 %, 95 % CI, 22.26–25.15 %), possibly due to presence of nutrients or heteroatoms in BDCDs and HCDs (Cheng et al., 2023). Specifically, N-doped CDs, N and S co-doped CDs, N and Mg co-doped CDs, N and P co-doped CDs accounted for 50.60 %, 12.00 %, 19.20 %, and 7.70 % of HCDs, respectively. The presence of N can optimize CDs structure, increase electron supply, and facilitate efficient separation of electrons and holes for effective light conversion and electron donation (Wang et al., 2021a). Besides an increase in photosynthesis, BDCDs also improve the growth indicators (+35.89 %, 95 % CI, 33.09–38.69 %) and nutrient and quality (+29.43 %, 95 % CI, 27.12–31.73 %). BDCDs are considered environmentally friendly and share similar properties with chemical CDs, such as strong photoluminescence emission, chemical stability, broad excitation wavelength, and tunable emission wavelength (Zhao et al., 2015). Moreover, BDCDs have the potential to be less toxic and more biocompatible (Wareing et al., 2021). The positive effects of BDCDs may be attributed to the nutrients contained in BDCDs, which favor the regulation of plant physiological processes (Cheng et al., 2023; Meng et al., 2019). For example, Abu Salha et al. (2023) suggested that the Piper betle-derived CDs have positive effects on the growth of strawberries(Abu Salha et al., 2023).

The significant optical property of CDs is PL. The PL color trend indicated that red exhibited the highest increase at 34.77 % (95 % CI, 32.30–37.24 %), followed by blue at 21.23 % (95 % CI, 21.40–22.05 %), and yellow at 9.52 % (95 % CI, 7.08–11.97 %) (Fig. 2b). Previous studies have suggested that due to the Emerson effect, red light has a superior effect of photosynthesis rate compared with blue light (Zhen and van Iersel, 2017). This could explain our results indicating that the red PL color exhibited the highest promotion effect in plant photosynthesis (red, +39.49 %, 95 % CI, 35.83–43.15 %; blue, +22.76 %, 95 % CI, 21.45–24.06 %; yellow, +16.92 %, 95 % CI, 13.01–20.84 %). Li et al. reinforce this conclusion by demonstrating that red-emitting CDs can function as efficient light-harvesting agents, thus enhance photosynthesis (Li et al., 2020a). Besides, the red PL color also showed advantage on other plant responses, such as growth indicators (+34.85 %, 95 % CI, 31.21–38.49 %) and antioxidant response (+31.20 %, 95 % CI, 24.53–37.87 %). In fact, the capability of red-emitting CDs to minimize autofluorescence interference from plant tissues could be the factor making them particularly well-suited for biological applications (Chiu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019).

According to Fig. 2c, the application methods is an essential factor in determining the impact of CDs on plant promotion. The results indicate that soil application (+36.05 %, 95 % CI, 32.45–39.64 %) and foliar application (+33.93 %, 95 % CI, 32.74–35.12 %) result in greater positive effects on plant relative to irrigation (+25.47 %, 95 % CI, 23.62–27.32 %) and hydroponic (+12.69 %, 95 % CI, 11.65–13.73 %). Soil application had the most positive effects on photosynthesis (+41.59 %), though this result is based on a limited number of data pairs (n = 9) and associated with large 95 % confidence interval (95 % CI, 30.84–52.33 %). Taking a broader perspective, soil application and foliar application appears to generally outperform root entry methods (irrigation and hydroponics). The positive effects observed from foliar application may be attributed to the large and efficient surface area of leaves, facilitating rapid absorption and response to the applied CDs (Hong et al., 2021; Ishfaq et al., 2022). The benefits of soil application can be attributed to the good dispersion of CDs in soil, particularly at low concentrations(Wang et al., 2017). The unique nanostructure, binding affinity, and other physicochemical properties of CDs are believed to stimulate enzyme activities, improve C and N turnovers, and increase the relative abundance and diversity of rhizosphere soil microorganisms (Achari and Kowshik, 2018; Chen et al., 2022b).

The effect of the application level of CDs (Fig. 2d) is consistent with “low promotion, high inhibition” effects. Concentrations lower than 10 ppm induced the promotion of photosynthesis (+35.11 %, 95 % CI, 32.96–37.26 %), nutrient and quality (+36.52 %, 95 % CI, 32.95–40.09 %), and growth indicators (+31.70 %, 95 % CI, 29.51–33.89 %), which was greater compared with other concentration levels. It should be noted that in soil (for soil application), ppm is equivalent to mg/kg, whereas in an aquatic solution (for irrigation, foliar application, and hydroponics), it denotes mg/L. This observation indicates that the behavior of CDs is similar to that of other engineered nanomaterials (ENMs), such as CeO2, CuO, and ZnO, which also demonstrated a trend of “low promoting and high inhibiting” effects in our previous research (Wang et al., 2020). Several studies demonstrated that high doses of CDs can induce toxic effects through the generation of reactive oxygen species, lipid peroxidation, and damage to proteins and DNA (Chen et al., 2022a; Havrdova et al., 2016; Kou et al., 2021).

In most cases, variations in synthesis conditions significantly influence the properties of CDs (Ma et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). As depicted in Fig. S3, CDs synthesized by electrolysis had the greatest positive effects on plants (+53.97 %, 95 % CI, 47.49–60.43 %), followed by electrochemical synthesis method (+28.28 %, 95 % CI, 23.74–32.83 %), hydrothermal synthesis method (+22.72 %, 95 % CI, 21.90–23.55 %), and microwave synthesis method (+15.89 %, 95 % CI, 14.16–17.62 %). The synthesis method significantly influences the properties of CDs; for instance, the electrochemical method yields CDs with a size range from 6 to 8 nm, while the microwave-assisted method yields CDs with a size near 4.51 nm (Singh et al., 2018). The connection between synthesis methods and the properties of CDs is the primary reason for the differential impact of various synthesized CDs on plant response (Ge et al., 2021). Overall, the properties of CDs, including their type, PL color, and synthesis method, as well as the experimental conditions such as application level and method, have significant impacts on plant responses. Our meta-analysis indicates that the utilization of red-emitting bio-derived CDs through soil or foliar application at a low concentration (<10 ppm) led to the most beneficial effects.

Several ML models (RF, GB, SVM, LR) were built using a dataset compiled from the meta-analysis. Initially, all features were included in constructing the ML models, and the feature importance are depicted in Fig. S4. To simplify the model and eliminate redundant features, sequential backward selection (SBS) was employed. The updated ML model performance for various plant responses, including growth indicators (GI), nutrient and quality (NuQ), photosynthesis (PSN), and antioxidant response (AR) are presented in Table S3-S6. The LR and SVM showed low R2 (GI, 0.25–0.64; NuQ, 0.23–0.40; PSN, 0.06–0.25; AR, 0.18–0.53) and high RMSE (GI, 0.24–0.50; NuQ, 0.24–0.28; PSN, 0.27–0.30; AR, 2.35–3.12), indicating poor performances compared with other ML models (RF and GB). This is consistent with Deng et al. (2021) whose results showed that RF and GB outperformed LR and SVM models in dealing with complex and nonlinear problems (Almeida, 2002; Deng et al., 2021). Subsequent feature analysis was thus conducted using RF and GB models.

The actual and predicted values of GI, NuQ, PSN, and AR were compared. Results for the RF model are shown in Fig. 3a–b and Fig. S5a-b, and for the GB model in Fig. S6a-S6b and Fig. S7a-S7b.The residuals analysis for the RF model is shown in Fig. S8-S11. GI was predicted with the highest accuracy, followed by PSN and NuQ. The model for AR displayed low accuracy, which is not surprising, considering (i) the complex and multilevel network that drive AR (Jones and Smirnoff, 2005; Vreeburg and Fry, 2005) and (ii) the limited dataset available for training the model (Raghunath et al., 2018).

Some discrepancies between the data points and the reference lines (y = x) can be attributed to the heterogeneity of published data (Danai-Varsou et al., 2023), but they also highlight that some features that affect plant response were not addressed in the literature (Li et al., 2023). Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) values were employed to interpret the relative contributions of each feature to the overall predictions of the model (Fig. 3c–d, Fig. S5c–d, Fig. S6c–d, Fig. S7c–d). When points with low feature values (blue) are primarily found on the left side, and points with high feature values (red) are mainly on the right side, this suggests a positive correlation between the feature and the predicted plant response – higher feature values are advantageous for positive plant response (Lundberg and Lee, 2017). Conversely, if points with higher values (red) tend to be on the left, and/or those with lower values (blue) are on the right, it shows a negative correlation between the feature and predicted plant response (Lundberg and Lee, 2017). The mean absolute Shapley (MAS) values were also calculated to quantify the overall importance of each descriptor (Fig. S12-S13). The trends of variable importance with number of trees are shown in Fig. S14-S17. The SHAP and MAS values identified size and dose as important factors in predicting plant responses across multiple aspects (GI, NuQ, PSN, and AR). When looking into more details, the single emission peak (Ems.s) was important for GI, humidity, Ems.s and stress were crucial for NuQ, and Abs.s and growth time were significant features for PSN. The contribution of one descriptor can be influenced by other descriptors. For instance, partial dependence plots (PDP) show strong interactions between CDs dose and size (Fig. 4c–f and Fig. S18). While the effect of dose “low promotion, high inhibition” is well accepted and confirmed in our meta-analysis above, the impact of the CDs size on plant response has been mostly overlooked. The results from our models indicate that lower concentration and smaller sizes are more favorable for most plant indicators (<161 ppm and <6 nm for GI, <308 ppm and 3–6 nm for NuQ, <491 ppm and <7 nm for PSN, <235 ppm and <5 nm for AR). Previous studies have demonstrated that smaller CDs are more likely to penetrate cells (Du et al., 2023) and stimulate the production of ROS (Du et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2021), which aligns with our research findings. Although the results suggest that size is a significant factor, it does not necessarily imply a direct causal link. Given that CDs are usually synthesized at small sizes and are employed in agricultural settings within biosystems, their size may rapidly change within these environments. Therefore, it is conceivable that the size of CDs directly impacts various properties that consequently influence plant responses. For example, the absorption and emission characteristics of CDs are size-dependent (Saheeda et al., 2019). For instance, an increase of CDs size from 1.2 to 3.8 nm can result in a red-shift of the emission from the ultraviolet to the near infrared (Li et al., 2010). This indicates that changes in CDs size can alter their physicochemical properties (such as Ems.s), thereby impacting properties like PL and quantum yield (QY) (Holá et al., 2017), ultimately affecting plant growth and development. When the CDs size is small (<7 nm), emission peak values are apparent within the range of 405–475 and 610–670 nm, indicating the effectiveness of both wavelengths in promoting plant growth and development (Fig. S19). One possible mechanism is that the specific emissions characteristics of CDs interact with plant receptors or signaling pathways, thus impacting processes related to growth (Cheng et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021a).

Illumination is an important factor influencing plant response, with a noted positive correlation between illumination and the impact of CDs on GI (Fig. 3c). However, when considering the interaction between illumination and photoperiod, the optimal values for GI fall within the range of −0.15 to −0.4 (290 to 330 μmol·m2·s−1) for illumination and − 0.2 to 0.4 (12 to 14 h) for photoperiod (Fig.S20). Notably, when illumination levels reached approximately 0.3 (400 μmol·m2·s−1), they were found to have a beneficial effect on PSN (Fig. S21). These results demonstrate that the variations in global climate patterns can affect the efficacy of CDs application. Moreover, the luminescent properties of CDs will be influenced by environmental factors such as humidity, temperature, and UV radiation (Jung et al., 2021). Prior studies have shown that changes in pH environment can impact the PL intensity of CDs (Saheeda et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2015); the temperature and humidity can affect both the size aggregation and quantum yield (QY) properties of CDs (Huang et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2017), which in turn affect their photonic generation efficiency. Therefore, the diversity in global climate distribution can affect the properties of CDs, subsequently influencing their photonic generation efficiency, photosynthesis (Ai et al., 2021), and ultimately impacting yield and quality (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021a). This influence of climate-related variables on the promotion effects of CDs highlights the significance of further global prediction to identify the areas where the application of CDs in agriculture is expected to yield significant benefits.

Overall, our analysis demonstrates that smaller sized CDs (<7 nm) induced stronger positive effects on plant responses, much likely via size-dependent properties of CDs, such as single emission peak (Ems.s) and single absorption peak (Abs.s), that influenced intermediary variables like PL and QY. More specifically, we thus recommend the synthesis of small CDs with Ems.s falling within the range of 405–475 and 610–670 nm. Furthermore, among all climate-related factors, illumination was the most impactful. Hence, future research should carefully consider CDs performances under different illumination conditions to ensure CDs broad applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Global prediction of CDs application efficiency in agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: Our study has revealed that climate and soil conditions affect the performance of CDs. A global map of spatial performance patterns was used to pinpoint the regions where the highest performance levels may be anticipated. The individual plant response (GI, NuQ, PSN, AR) to CDs in current and future scenario are shown in Fig. 5 , while the mean plant response (integration of GI, NuQ, PSN, and AR) is presented in Fig. S22. The distribution of predicted plant responses in current scenario indicated that the application of CDs could significantly improve plant GI (1.24 ± 0.15 times), NuQ (1.10 ± 0.10 times), PSN (1.30 ± 0.04 times), and AR (1.39 ± 0.29 times), globally (Fig. S23). Compared with current scenario, the future results show slight changes. For instance, NuQ and AR significantly increased by 7 % and 21 %, respectively, while PSN decreased by 3 % (Fig. S23). The disparity of other plant response (GI, NUQ, and AR) in the northern and southern hemispheres could be attributed to climate-related factors, such as night temperature, daytime temperature, and illumination (Fig. S12–13). Significant improvements in plant response with the application of CDs were observed in major agricultural countries, including the US, India, Canada, and China. As a result of CDs application, the plant responses of NuQ in the US, China, and Canada would increase from 1.11 times to 1.17 times, from 1.09 to 1.15 times, and from 1.12 to 1.28 times, respectively (Fig. S24). These results indicate the remarkable global potential effects of CDs application on plant growth and development in the face of changing climate conditions, suggesting that CDs may have widespread applicability.

CDs possess significant potential in agriculture to improve the growth and production of plants (Li et al., 2020c). It has been extensively reported that CDs-based nanomaterials can effectively reduce the environmental impact of agricultural production while concurrently maintaining or enhancing crop quality and yields (An et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020b; Wang et al., 2021a). Therefore, by leveraging the integration of meta-analysis and ML, this research comprehensively explores the response of crop quality and yield to CDs application, while also quantifying the relative importance of various influencing factors. Our meta-analysis of 2564 data points reveals that the soil and foliar application of red-emitting bio-derived CDs at a moderate concentration (<10 ppm) leads to the most beneficial effects on plant growth. The ML results suggest that customized synthesis of CDs is necessary to meet specific requirements of crop response. For example, low concentration and small-sized CDs (<491 ppm and < 7 nm) have proven to be more effective in promoting GI, NuQ, PSN, and AR, with a difference optimal size range for these functions (<161 ppm and < 6 nm for GI, <308 ppm and 3–6 nm for PSN). This size-dependent effect can be attributed to their interaction with Ems.s. During stress conditions such as drought, higher concentrations of CDs should be considered for application. Among varying climate conditions, illumination exerts the most significant influence on plant growth and development. The global application, based on climate and soil conditions, reveals that CDs have widespread applicability, and their application enhance key plant parameters including GI, PSN, NuQ, and AR, signifying their positive impact on plant growth. Optimizing properties of CDs and experimental conditions, such as size, dose, and Ems.s, can enhance the application effectiveness of CDs. These findings strongly support the potential of CDs as effective tools in agriculture. Our study on understanding the impact of CDs on plant response can guide future optimization of these nanomaterials for use in agriculture.

While this study did not consider the cost-effectiveness and environmental impacts of CDs, several studies have demonstrated that CDs are a cost-effective approach in bolstering crop yields and long-term profitability for farmers (Dong et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2022). The affordability, low toxicity, and ease of large-scale application indicate the significant potential for widespread use of CDs in the agricultural industry. More work is also necessary to elucidate the specific mechanisms underlying the diverse effects of various application methods and to optimize application techniques to maximize the benefits of CDs in plant growth and development. Moreover, the formation of biocoronas around nanomaterials significantly impacts their interfacial properties and the activity of biomolecules (Li et al., 2022; Wheeler et al., 2021). This process triggers a range of biological effects, highlighting the significance of comprehending biocorona formation and its relevance to CD type, properties, plant species, and exposure conditions. Moving forward, future research should also explore the degradation mechanisms of various types of CDs and the potential release of dopant atoms (heteroatoms).
All computer codes for the analysis of the data are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

J.L. and C.W. designed the experiments. J.L. performed meta-analysis, machine learning, and finished the manuscript. X.L., M.K., L.Y., B.C., C.W., Z.W., and B.X. helped with the discussion and revised the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and commented on this work.

Jing Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Xiaona Li: Writing – review & editing. Melanie Kah: Writing – review & editing. Le Yue: Writing – review & editing. Bingxu Cheng: Writing – review & editing. Chuanxi Wang: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhenyu Wang: Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Baoshan Xing: Writing – review & editing.
